coredata.dc:title: Chemosensing properties and logic gate behaviors of graphene quantum dot-appended terpyridine
coredata.dc:creator: Kang, Si-Yuan
Yin, Hong
Zhang, Kai-Qi
Chen, Xiaobo
Wang, Ke-Zhi
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dot-covalently appended terpyridine, GQDs–tpy, was synthesized and characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, FTIR spectroscopy and transmission electron microscopy. GQDs–tpy was found to act as multifunctional chemosensors: a highly selective colorimetric chemosensor for Fe2+ as evidenced by an obvious color change from colorless to pink, and a typical fluorescence enhanced probe for Zn2+ over 13 metal cations even in practical water samples. Moreover, two-input XOR, INHIBIT and IMPICATION logic gates as well as four-input OR and NOR logic gates were constructed according to the characteristic responses of GQDs-tpy to a sequence of cations.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Terpyridine
Chemosensor
Optical sensor
Logic gate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Experimental details for optically sensing studies
Detection of Fe2+ and Zn2+ in real water samples
Multiple logic gates based on GQDs-tpy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Molecular structures of some representative Fe2+ and/or Zn2+ chemosensors carrying free terpyridine group.

(a) TEM images with scale bar of 5 nm of GQDs (a) and GQDs-tpy (b); Size histograms of GQDs (c) and GQDs-tpy (d). XRD pattern of GQDs-tpy (e) and FT-IR spectra (f) of GQDs-tpy (top), tpyCOOH (middle) and GQDs (bottom).

Survey XPS spectrum (a), high-resolution C1s spectrum (b) and high-resolution N1 s spectrum (c) of GQDs; Survey XPS spectrum (d), high-resolution C1s spectrum (e), and high-resolution N1 s spectrum (f) of GQDs-tpy.

Photograph (a) of GQDs-tpy (40 mg/L)/cations (20 μM) mixtures in Hepes buffer solution (20 mM, pH = 7.2) taken under day light. Changes in absorption spectra (b) and fluorescence spectra (c) (λ ex = 280 nm) of GQDs-tpy (40 mg/L) upon Fe2+ (0–13 μM) titrations in Hepes buffer solution (20 mM, pH = 7.2). The insets show the changes in absorbances at 565 nm or emission intensities at 372 nm vs. ferrous ion concentrations.

(a) The bar graphs showing emission intensity ratios of GQDs-tpy (40 mg/L) in the presence and the absence of the metal cations (20 μM) tested. Changes in absorption spectra (b) and fluorescence spectra (c) (λex = 330 nm) of GQDs-tpy (40 mg/L) upon Zn2+ (0–13 μM) titrations in Hepes buffer solution (20 mM, pH = 7.2).

(a) XOR and OR logic gates based on GQDs-tpy constructed by monitoring the absorbances at 273 and 324 nm respectively, and (b) corresponding truth tables; (c) Histograms of output signal and (d) the combined logic circuit of XOR and OR logic gates.

(a) Changes in UV–vis spectra of GQDs-tpy with 20 μM of Zn2+ and Fe2+ being as two inputs, and (b) the corresponding truth tables of Inhibit and OR logic gates when monitoring the absorbances at 563 and 324 nm respectively. (c) Histogram of output signals at 324 and 563 nm and (d) the combined logic circuit of INHIBIT and OR logic gates.

(a) Changes in fluorescence spectra of GQDs-tpy with 20 μM of Zn2+ and Fe2+ being as two inputs, and (b) corresponding truth table of IMP logic gate when monitoring the emission intensities at 370 nm. (c) Histogram of output signals at 370 nm and (d) the logic circuit of IMP logic gate.

(a) Histograms of the output signals of GQDs-tpy from sixteen possible input combination. (b) Corresponding truth table of the combined four inputs. (c) Schematic representation of a logic circuit of four-input OR and NOR logic gates.
Comparisons of colorimetric optical sensing properties of ferrous chemosensors carrying free terpyridine group.
Comparisons of emission sensing properties of Zn2+ chemosensors carrying free terpyridine group.
Recovery study of Fe2+ in pond water and tap water.
Recovery study of Zn2+ in pond water and tap water.

head.ce:title: Chemosensing properties and logic gate behaviors of graphene quantum dot-appended terpyridine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Key Laboratory of Energy Conversion and Storage Materials and College of Chemistry, Beijing Normal University, Beijing 100875, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Key Laboratory of Energy Conversion and Storage Materials and College of Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Applied Optics Beijing Area Major Laboratory and Physics Department, Beijing Normal University, Beijing 100875, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Applied Optics Beijing Area Major Laboratory and Physics Department

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dot-covalently appended terpyridine, GQDs–tpy, was synthesized and characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, FTIR spectroscopy and transmission electron microscopy. GQDs–tpy was found to act as multifunctional chemosensors: a highly selective colorimetric chemosensor for Fe2+ as evidenced by an obvious color change from colorless to pink, and a typical fluorescence enhanced probe for Zn2+ over 13 metal cations even in practical water samples. Moreover, two-input XOR, INHIBIT and IMPICATION logic gates as well as four-input OR and NOR logic gates were constructed according to the characteristic responses of GQDs-tpy to a sequence of cations.

ce:section.ce:para: Chemosensors capable of detecting metal cations have attracted considerable attention due to the facts that the metal cations play essential roles in industrial, environmental and biological processes [1–15]. Among these metal cations, the two most abundant transitionmetal ions of Fe2+ and Zn2+ take vital positions in human bodies and other mammals, such as participating in various biology processes including oxygen transport and catalytic cofactor for many proteins [2–16]. Therefore sensitive and selective recognition of biologically important Fe2+ and Zn2+ is particularly significant and has attracted much interest. As for optical sensing community, naked-eye colorimetric detection of certain species is the most appealing technique, due to its advantage of simplicity without using any spectroscopic instrumentation [7–9]. On the other hand, comparing with easily attainable fluorescence quenching mode [10–12], the fluorescence “turn-on” mode seems to be more preferable which could not only lessen the interference of background, but also improve the selectivity of the target analytes [17]. Although a number of colorimetric and emission sensors toward Fe2+ and Zn2+ have been developed [2–10,13–21], some vital problems with respect to poor selectivity and sensitivity are also existing. Meier and Kimura coworkers reported a terpyridine-based chemosensor toward Fe(II), but it has a poor selectivity [13,14]. Yang and coworker reported a terpyridine moiety-containing sensor, which is only capable of detecting the total amount of Fe(II + III) [15]. Some fluorogenic sensors toward Zn2+ previously reported, were often interfered by other metal ions with similar properties especially Cd2+ which is in the same group of the periodic table [16–18]. The sensing is preferable to perform in fully aqueous system, which is particularly important for application in living systems. However, in some cases the optical sensors have to be utilized in organic solvents [18–21], limiting their applications in biological systems.

Terpyridine moiety deserves concern because of its avid metal coordination capacity and characteristic photophysical properties as well as highly tunable metal ion sensing properties [14]. However, currently available terpyridine-based chemosensors mainly dealt with purely organic compounds [13–34] and metal complexes [3,8,35] (see Fig. 1 for their molecular structures) with some disadvantages such as inconvenient synthesis or purification procedures and limited water solubility and sensing performance [8,9,13–26]. On the other hand, chemosensors grafted on quantum dots [7,11,12,17] and silica [28], polymer [14,29] and other nanoparticles [27,42] were reported to be capable of enhancing not only sensing performance but also water-dispensability of the chemosensors. Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have aroused increasing attention due to their excellent water solubility, high biocompatibility and desirable optical properties [37–44]. Vast majority of reported GQDs or carbon dots are related to experimental synthesis, while just a few were focused on ion sensing [37–40].

On the other hand, molecular systems exhibiting sequential advanced logic functions have attracted tremendous attention for information processing and data storage [45,46]. Ion-responsive sensors d isplay a remarkable application to mimic the Boolean arithmetic, because of their high target-selectivity input and abundant and obvious spectral signal output [47–51]. Only a few of these sensors are based on graphene quantum dots triggered completely by metal cations [37,52]. Keeping these in mind, we design a terpyridyl-grafted GQDs, that acts as highly sensitive and selective colorimetric and fluorescence “turn-on” sensor in aqueous solution and capable of discriminating Fe(II) and Zn(II) from Fe(III), Cd(II) or the other metal cations tested. Compared with analogous materials reported, GQDs-tpy showed desirable water solubility, relatively low limitation of detection (LOD) and broader quantitative detection range. Furthermore, utilizing its excellent spectral properties, two-input XOR, INHIBIT and IMPICATION logic gates as well as four-input OR and NOR gates were constructed.

GQDs [53] and 2,6-di(pyridin-2-yl)isonicotinic acid (TpyCOOH) [54] were synthesized according to previously published protocols. The perchlorate salts of metal cations (Na+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Hg2+ and Ag+) used in optically sensing experiments, were purchased from ACROS, and stored in a vacuum desiccator. 3-(Aminopropyl)trimethoxysilane (APTMS) used for covalent modification of GQDs, was also purchased from ACROS. All other reagents for the synthesis and the surface modification of GQDs were obtained commercially, and were used without further purification.

TEM observations were performed on JEOL JEM 2100 operating at 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were carried out on PANalytical X'Pert Pr MPD with Cu K α radiation. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were measured on an Axis Ultra X-ray photoelectron spectrometer from Thermo Fisher using Al K α irradiation with an ESCA-250 system. The IR spectra were obtained on a Nicolet Avatar 360 FT-IR spectrometer as KBr disks. The absorption spectra were collected on a UV-2450 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu). Fluorescence spectra were recorded using a Cary Eclipse spectrofluorophotometer (VARIAN) at room temperature.

GQDs-tpy was synthesized according to a modified literature method [55] via two steps as shown in Fig. 2 . Firstly, APTMS was covalently anchored on GQDs. GQDs (0.3 g) was dispersed in 180 mL ethanol by sonication for 0.5 h (25 W, 40 kHz). The amino-functionalized GQDs was achieved by stirring the suspension with excess APTMS (0.5 g, 2.71 mmol) and refluxed for 6 h. The resulting suspension was filtered, washed with ethanol and vacuum-dried to afford GQDs-NH2 as a black solid with a yield of 75.6%. Secondly, GQDs-NH2 was covalently functionalized by terpyridyl. Briefly, TpyCOOH (94 mg, 0.3 mmol) was dissolved in SOCl2 (10 mL), the resulting mixture was stirred under reflux for 1 h under N2 followed by removal of SOCl2 under reduced pressure. To the residue was added anhydrous THF (10 mL), Et3N (2 mL) and GQDs-NH2 (50 mg) subsequently, followed by refluxing for 12 h. The reaction mixture was then filtered and washed with CH2Cl2 to afford GQDs-tpy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental details for optically sensing studies
ce:section.ce:para: All of the experiments for the interaction of the GQDs-tpy with various cations (Na+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Hg2+ and Ag+) were investigated in Hepes buffer solution (20 mM, pH 7.2). A stock solution of GQDs-tpy (1 mg/mL) was prepared with Hepes buffer (20 mM, pH 7.2) and diluted up to 40 mg/L on testing. When performing the titrations, the same volume of metal cations was injected into reference cuvettes to eliminate the absorption of cations or their counter anions.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of GQDs and GQDs-tpy are compared in Fig. 3a and b. It was found that the particles of GQDs were uniformly dispersed and their average lateral size was 3.2 ± 0.6 nm (Fig. 3c). After a covalent grafting of terpyridyl moiety to GQDs, the average diameters of the GQDs-tpy were increased to 4.0 ± 0.6 nm (Fig. 3d). The high resolution TEM image (Fig. 3a inset) clearly unveiled the crystal lattice of GQDs, which consisted of almost defect-free graphene single crystals with a spacing of 0.22 nm corresponding to that of graphene (100) planes [56]. XRD study of the GQDs–tpy was also carried out and the pattern (see Fig. 3e) shows that GQDs–tpy is crystalline as demonstrated by a characteristic broad (002) peak centered at ca. 26.83°. The surface functionalization was also characterized through Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and XPS spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 3f, GQDs exhibited characteristic stretching vibration peak of OH at 3433 cm−1, skeletal vibration of CC in aromatic ring at 1570 cm−1 and stretching vibration of CO at 1288 cm−1 [57], confirming the existence of hydroxyl groups but inexistence of carboxyl groups on the surface of GQDs, which provides a platform for further surface modification with -NH2 by APTMS. After the two-step functionalization, the GQDs were finally decorated with terpyridyl through the reaction between the carboxyl groups in TpyCOOH and the amino groups on the surfaces of GQDs-NH2. In contrast to the GQDs, the presence of the peaks at 2925 and 2848 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of GQDs–tpy, were assigned to the amide NH, and the peak at 1670 cm−1 is attributable to the carbonyl stretching vibration of the amides. The successful linkage of terpyridyl to GQDs was further verified by XPS. XPS spectrum of GQDs (Fig. 2a) shows the presence of C and O as the prominent elements as evidenced by strong C1s and O1s peaks at 284 and at 530 eV respectively. Na Auger peak at 497 eV was originated from NaOH employed in the neutralization process of the GQDs precursor. In contrast to negligibly weak N1 s signal in the survey spectrum of GQDs (Fig. 4a), much stronger N1 s signal at ~400 eV was observed in that of GQDs-tpy (Fig. 4d) due to the linkage of N-containing APTES and tpy onto the surface of DQDs. The high-resolution C1s spectrum of GQDs (Fig. 4b) displayed only two deconvoluted peaks: strong one at 284.8 eV and the other distinguishable one at 288.2 eV that are attributable to CC and the CO respectively. Besides the signal of CC, the high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 4e) of GQDs-tpy could be deconvoluted into three additional distinct components centered at 286.0, 286.4 and 288.7 eV, which are attributed to –C=N-, CO, O=C-NH- respectively. In contrast to the high-resolution N1 s spectrum of GQDs (Fig. 4c), the N1 s spectrum (Fig. 4f) of GQDs-tpy revealed the presence of -N=C (terpyridine), -HN-C=O (amide) and NH3 + centered at 398.9, 399.8 and 400.6 eV respectively. Taken together, the results confirmed the successful synthesis of the GQDs–tpy [58].

Although precursor TpyCOOH is almost insoluble in water, its covalent hybrid material to GQDs, GQDs-tpy, is very soluble in water, making it possible to apply GQDs-tpy in fully aqueous environment for following cation optical sensing study. As shown in Fig. 5a, noticeable color change from colorless to pink was observed when GQDs-tpy (40 mg/L) was treated with 20 μM of excess ferrous ion, while no color changes were observed when equivalent other cations (Na+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Mn2+,Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Hg2+ and Ag+) were added, indicating that GQDs-tpy acted as an efficient and selective colorimetric sensor for ferrous ion.

Detailed UV–Vis absorption spectrophotometric titrations were carried out to evaluate the recognition ability of GQDs-tpy toward Fe2+. As shown in Fig. 5b, with the successive titrations of Fe2+, two new absorption bands centered at 585 nm (ε = 1.31 × 104 M−1·cm−1) and 325 nm (ε = 2.25 × 104 M−1·cm−1) appeared, and the absorbance of the band at 310 nm decreased gradually until disappeared. The distinct color change was ascribed to the development of metal-to-ligand charge-transfer (MLCT) band at 585 nm due to the Fe2+ coordination to terpyridyl in GQDs-tpy [59]. The colorimetric limitation of detection (LOD) for Fe2+ was calculated using the equation [60].
LOD = 3σ / S
where σ is the standard deviation and S is the linear slope of the Fig. 5a inset. The LOD value of Fe2+ was determined to be 1.43 × 10−7 M. In contrast to some of terpyridine-containing chemosensors that were previously reported to respond to both Fe2+and Fe3+ [61], the color changes observed in GQDs-tpy when treated with only Fe2+ but not Fe3+ made GQDs-tpy to discriminate iron in oxidative states vividly to the naked eye. To determine the reaction mechanism of GQDs-tpy toward Fe2+, fluorescence emission spectra were also studied. Upon successive additions of Fe2+, the emission of the band at 372 nm (λex = 280 nm) declined in the intensities gradually until total quenching (Fig. 5c), indicative of evident quenching in the terpyridyl-associated 1π-π⁎ fluorescence by the Fe2+ coordination. Both Fig. 5b and c insets show that the concentration ranges of linear responses in the absorbances at 585 nm and emission intensities at 372 nm were from 0.1 to 10 μM, which provided the evidences for the quantitative determination of ferrous ion. As shown in Table 1 , most of previously reported Fe2+ optical sensors carrying free terpyridine group, had to be used in organic solvents, while GQDs-tpy could sense Fe2+ in purely aqueous solution. Fe2+ detection range (0–8.0 μM) and detection limit (DL) of 0.14 μM found for GQDs-tpy, are among the top class chemosensors as compared to those of the other Fe2+ chemosensors listed in Table 1 and a lower DL value of 0.02 μM noted for L6 [23]. It is noteworthy that the Fe2+ detection range and detection limit values for GQDs-tpy are improved to some extent as compared to a DL value of 0.26 μM and detection range 0–4.0 μM toward Fe2+ previously reported for the model hybrid material SiO
2
-tpy [36]. To determine the selectivity of GQDs-tpy toward Fe2+, cross-contamination assays were performed. As shown in Fig. S1, although the absorbance varied to some extents in the presence of other metal ions, the peak around 585 nm was still discerned with ease, indicating the high selectivity of GQDs-tpy toward colorimetric sensing of Fe2+.

The results of fluorescence titrations (λ ex = 280 nm) of GQDs–tpy (40 mg/L) with different metal cations were shown in Fig. 6a. Additions of Na+ and Ba2+ only induced very minor decreases in emission of GQDs–tpy; while GQDs–tpy was subjected to moderate fluorescence quenching to varying extents when exposed to Mn2+, Fe3+, Cd2+ and Ag+; Obviously, upon additions of Cu2+, Fe2+, Hg2+, Co2+ and Ni2+, fluorescence emission intensities of GQDs–tpy were completely quenched; Interestingly, additions of Zn2+ induced significant enhancements in the fluorescent intensities of GQDs–tpy, indicating that GQDs–tpy acted as a highly selective fluorescent chemosensor to Zn2+, although this sensing action has a disadvantage of high-energy ultraviolet emission rather than low-energy visible or near-infrared luminescence. It should be noted that this highly selective Zn2+ luminescence sensor operated by Zn2+-induced luminescence enhancement but Cd2+-,Hg2+- and other common transition metal ion-induced emission quenching is novel as compared to model hybrid material SiO2-tpy [36], and is rare and similar to the behavior of chemosensor L8 [31]. It is very challenging to design chemosensors of this kind that can distinguish multiple analytes with similar chemical properties, e.g., Cd(II) and Zn(II) are in the same group of the periodic table and have similar spectral properties [16,30,31,33,34]. To determine the coordination behaviors between Zn2+ and GQDs–tpy, detailed studies were carried out. Fig. 6b and c show absorption and emission responses of GQDs-tpy (40 mg/L) toward Zn2+ in Hepes buffer solution (20 mM, pH = 7.2). Upon successive additions of Zn2+, the absorption intensities of the band at 295 nm decreased gradually until disappeared with appearance of a new peak at 330 nm, and a clear isosbestic point at 310 nm appeared (Fig. 6b). The binding stoichiometry and binding constant K of GQDs-tpy with different cations were evaluated through the Benesi–Hildebrand Eqs. (1) and (2) [62–64].
(1)
1
A −
A 0
=
1
A ∞
−
A 0
1
K
M
n
+ 1
(2)
1
I −
I 0
=
1
I ∞
−
I 0
1
K
M
n
+ 1

Here A 0 and I 0 are absorbance and fluorescent intensity of GQDs-tpy in the absence of metal cations; A ∞ and I ∞ are absorbance and fluorescence intensity of GQDs-tpy at the saturation binding. A and I represent absorbance and fluorescent intensity of GQDs-tpy in the presence of the cations under unsaturation binding condition; [M] and n are cation concentration and binding stoichiometry (cation:terpyridy moiety in GQDs-tpy) and binding constant respectively. The inset of Fig. 6b shows a plot of 1/(A − A 0) at 330 nm against 1/[Zn2+], which gives a 1:1 binding stoichiometry and a binding constant K of 8.125 × 104 M−1 for Zn2+ coordination to terpyridine in GQDs-tpy [50,51]. Moreover, successive additions of Zn2+ elicited obvious emission enhancements of GQDs–tpy with a factor of 1.2 at λ e x = 280 nm (Fig. 6a) or 2.5-folds at λ e x = 310 nm (Fig. 4c). The inset of Fig. 6c shows the linear growths of the fluorescence intensities against the concentrations of Zn2+ from 0.1 to 8 μM, providing the evidences for the quantitative determination of zinc ion. Although GQDs-tpy acted as a fluorescence “turn on” type Zn2+ sensor, the linear range and DL values were only moderate as compared with those of some previously reported representative Zn2+ fluorescence sensors carrying free terpyridine group (see Table 2 ); however, it is noteworthy that Zn2+ detection limit compares favorably with those reported for the other Zn2+ fluorescence sensors. However, it should be pointed out that the Zn2+ fluorescent sensing could be seriously interference by the presence of equiv. amount of Co2+, Fe2+, Cu2+ and Cd2+, because GQDs-tpy/Zn2+ fluorescence was evidenced by the presence of equiv. amount of these ions as shown in Fig. S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe2+ and Zn2+ in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para:
To illustrate the feasibility of practical applications of GQDs–tpy for optical sensing of Fe2+ and Zn2+, GQDs–tpy was applied in the determination of these two cations in real water samples of pond water and tap water. The results are displayed in Tables 3 and 4
. It was found that the cation amounts determined by GQDs-tpy were similar to those of the spiked (added) and the recovery values were nearly 100%. It is suggested that GQDs–tpy be suited to detect Fe2+ and Zn2+ in real water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multiple logic gates based on GQDs-tpy
ce:section.ce:para: On the basis of the phenomenon that GQDs-tpy has displayed significant modulation of absorption and emission properties upon binding with some cations, several logic gates can be developed by the sequential combination of two or four cationic inputs with a fixed concentration of 20 μM. Binary digits (1, 0) were used to sign the presence and absence of these inputs, and high and low optical outputs respectively. The XOR gate is the most complicated and hard to crack, which plays a significant role in encryption and information security. Because of its difficulty of construction, only limited numbers of molecular XOR logic gates have been reported. Fortunately, both XOR and OR logic gates can be modeled when we designated Ni2+ (20 μM) and Fe2+ (20 μM) as two inputs, and the absorbance values at 273 and 324 nm as output signals, respectively. On basis of the changes in absorption spectra (Fig.7a), it can be observed that either of the two inputs was added (binary 0,1 and binary 1,0), the absorbance values at 272 nm were above an absorbance threshold of 0.4 (output 1) as shown in Fig. 7c left panel. However, as shown in Fig. 7c right panel, when the absorbances at 324 nm were monitored as output and an absorbance threshold was set at 0.3, except for the case that both Ni2+ (20 μM) and Fe2+ (20 μM) were absent, giving a solution absorbance below the threshold (binary 0), the other three cases that were in the absence of 20 μM of both Ni2+ and Fe2+ (binary 1, 1) and either Ni2+ alone (binary 1, 0) or Fe2+ alone (binary 0, 1), The truth table (Fig. 7b) and logic circuit (Fig. 7d) and, correspond to XOR and OR logic gates.

The INHIBIT gate is integrated of two basic logic gates: AND and NOT, which is of great importance due to its non-commutative behavior and indicating that one input own veto power over the output signal [65]. The combination of Zn2+ and Fe2+ two inputs can achieve this function. As shown in Fig. 8a, the presence of Zn2+ (20 μM) can totally inhibit the absorbance signal of Fe2+ (20 μM) at 563 nm. Co2+, Cu2+ and Hg2+ can also make up INHIBIT logic gates with Fe2+ (see Figs. S3–S5).

The IMPLICATION (IMP) gate contains three kinds of fundamental logic gates: AND, NOT and OR, which can exhibit competition of inputs and possibly apply in multiplexed detection of analytes. We observed that the fluorescence of GQDs-tpy was quenched by Fe2+, but protected by Zn2+. According to this phenomenon, an IMP logic gate was constructed with Fe2+ and Zn2+ as two inputs and the fluorescence at 372 nm as output signal (Fig. 9 ), and the fluorescence of GQDs-tpy could be quenched, namely the output “0”, only under the condition that Fe2+ added alone in the absence of Zn2+.

Based on the above-mentioned observations that the addition of Ni2+, Co2+, Hg2+ and Fe2+ all induce quenching in the fluorescence of GQDs-tpy and a new absorption peak to appear at 324 nm, a multiple logic gate containing OR and NOR logic gates was developed by these four ionic inputs (Fig. 10 ). In this context, we assigned equivalent Ni2+, Co2+, Hg2+ and Fe2+(20 μM) as In1, In2, In3 and In4, respectively and the absorbance value at 324 nm and the emission intensity at 370 nm as output 1 and output 2. On basis of the histogram of output signals (Fig. 10a) and the true table (Fig. 10b), OR and NOR logic gates were obtained and could be served as universal gates applied to create more complicated Boolean operations.

Terpyridyl moiety was covalently grafted to GQDs, which was verified by the increasing particle size shown in TEM and the appearance of −NH-C=O associated N1 s XPS peaks and FTIR absorption bands. TEM photographs showed that the resulting hybrid GQDs-tpy had a mean diameter of ca. 4.0 nm. Grafting of GQDs to terpyridine group renders resulting hybrid material GQDs-tpy highly water soluble and being of improved difunctional chemosensing properties as compared to the model hybrid material SiO2-tpy [36], namely, GQDs-tpy was evidenced to act as a highly sensitive and selective ferrous colorimetric chemosensor with detection limitations of ∼1.43 × 10−7 M, which compare favorably with most of previously reported colorimetric ferrous chemosensors carrying free tpy group, and a typical fluorimetric “turn on” probe for Zn2+ with a broad detection range of 0.1–8.0 μM and a low detection limit of 5.7 × 10−8 M. Moreover, multiple logic gates including XOR, INHIBIT and IMPICATION logic gates for two inputs as well as OR and NOR gates for four inputs can be constructed based on the abundant absorption and emission spectral properties. In view of combination of intriguing GQDs and versatile metal coordination capacity of free terpyridine group, this study paves the way to develop not only optical sensing, switching and logic gate molecular devices but also multifunctional metal complex-based hydrid nanomaterials.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Beijing Municipal Natural Science Foundation (2182028), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21541010), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China (2017TZ01) and Measurements Fund of Beijing Normal University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2019.02.014.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Near-infrared fluorescent probes with large stokes shifts for sensing Zn(II) ions in living cells
Highly sensitive and selective difunctional ruthenium(II) complex-based chemosensor for dihydrogen phosphate anion and ferrous cation
Synthesis, fluorescence-sensing and molecular logic of two water-soluble 1,8-naphthalimides
Synthesis of a single 1,8-naphthalimide fluorophore as a molecular logic lab for simultaneously detecting of Fe3+, Hg2+ and Cu2+
Grafting polyethylenimine with quinoline derivatives for targeted imaging of intracellular Zn2+ and logic gate operations
CdTe quantum dots (QDs) based kinetic discrimination of Fe2+ and Fe3+, and CdTe QDs-fenton hybrid system for sensitive photoluminescent detection of Fe2+
A functionalized ruthenium(II)-bis-terpyridine complex as a rod-like luminescent sensor of zinc(II)
A colorimetric sensor for Fe2+ ion
Fluorescent sensors for measuring metal ions in living systems
Ultrasensitive Pb2+ detection by glutathione-capped quantum dots
Highly sensitive synchronous fluorescence determination of mercury (II) based on the denatured ovalbumin coated CdTe QDs
Fluorescent sensing of transition metal ions based on the encapsulation of dithranol in a polymeric core shell architecture
Fluorescence chemosensor for metal ions using conjugated polymers
A highly selective colorimetric chemosensor for detecting the respective amounts of iron(II) and iron(III) ions in water
A phenylene–vinylene terpyridine conjugate fluorescent probe fordistinguishing Cd2+from Zn2+with high sensitivity and selectivity
Quantum dot-based “turn-on” fluorescent probe for detection of zinc and cadmium ions in aqueous media
